2k+ hours in to this game and 10k+ hours in to this franchise. This entire franchise is being subsidized by the in game gambling; i.e. loot crates & skins market. If the in game skins market wasn't worth millions of dollars, this game would have died 10-15 years ago. The actual gameplay is the same old and the competitive scene is more or less limited to pro leagues that take place on THIRD PARTY platforms like FaceIt. That's how bad the in game anti cheat is in this game, NOBODY that takes this game seriously plays competitively on it. Cheaters and hackers are so prevalent in game that third party platforms have their own market just based on superior anti cheat processes. This game is a sad, hollow shell of what it once was. And the developers know this; they just like making money because at the end of the day you have to buy this game (used to be $9.99) to even play on said third party platforms + millions in a market for in game skins. Very obviously, the developers spend more time and effort in creating new skins than working towards a fix for hackers in game or fixing the illogical matchmaking process. As a new player, you WILL go against more "smurfs" (experienced players that create new accounts or lose on purpose to derank to play in low level ranks to feel better about themselves) and hackers than actual other new players. This is because, as mentioned before, any experienced players that take this game seriously is not playing in game; they are playing on third party platforms like FaceIt. You will never achieve anything more than frustration trying to play this game standalone. Most players are usually in mod servers that aren't even related to the actual game, like zombie mods and zombie escape mods. This franchise used to be a leader in first person shooters, now its just a cash grab for developers trying to get as many people addicted to the in game gambling mechanics related to skins. Even as a free game, not worth your time, believe me. Unless you realllly think you wanna go "pro" for some odd reason or realllly like gambling.

I have played Counter-Strike for years and love the core gameplay mechanics, the updated Source 2 visuals, and the tactical depth. However, in its current state, Counter-Strike 2 is nearly unplayable in official matchmaking.

The cheater problem in Premier and Competitive modes has gotten completely out of control. Wallhacks, aimbots, and blatant spinbots appear in a staggering number of high-Elo and even casual matches. Valve’s VAC Live and Trust Factor systems are completely inadequate at stopping illicit software.

It is sad that players are forced to migrate to third-party platforms like FACEIT just to get a fair match with a working anti-cheat. I cannot recommend this game until Valve fundamentally overhauls their anti-cheat system.

Rating: Not Recommended

Look, there are a lot of problems with this game. Cheaters are everywhere, the economy is nuts as a new player, and even if you're playing in a lobby with legit players, the skill curve is absolutely diabolical. As of this review, I don't even have 30 hours on this game, but the problems are abundantly clear to me. However, that's not to say you shouldn't play this game.

Let's start with the negatives - 

-You will encounter cheaters in 60-80% of the lobbies you play in
-You WILL be called a slur at least once every few games
-It will likely take you 10 hours of playing to get your first kill
-There's a lot of general jankiness in the game, movement feels unclean, sometimes the scope feels off, etc
-Sound is off on a lot of guns and environments

Positives -

-It's really fun to play with a few friends on call
-With practice, the game can become incredibly satisfying
-If you like spending money, there are always new skins and cases coming out(I don't recommend cases though.)
-While gameplay can feel impossible at first, it is usually fair towards the player after practice
-Guns feel relatively realistic(apart from sound)
-Valve made maps are generally fair and not hard to figure out
-Premier is pretty fair towards the player if bought, offering reduced smurf and cheater encounters and weekly care packages

Things to be careful of:

Yes, cases are enticing. Who knows? You could roll a gold. However, in reality, this is an infinitesimally small chance. If you want a skin, DO NOT buy a case to get it. Just buy it off of the Steam market and be done. Also, don't spend an absurd amount of money on a skin because its "cool". There are so many cool skins you can find for 5 dollars or less, just buy one of those.

Overall, the game definitely has its flaws, but its a classic, and in the end, everything still runs pretty smooth and you can tune out a lot of its issues. I hope Valve will fix the cheating problem at some point, but that's a long shot. Even so, there's a reason this is still the top played game on Steam after so many years. 

Have a great day

Counter-Strike 2 is, in my opinion, one of the most frustrating games I have ever played. Many players believe that cheating is still a serious problem. Because of this, some matches feel unfair and disappointing.

Another issue is that players often complain about bugs, server problems, and matchmaking. Sometimes it feels like skill does not matter as much as it should. When you spend time practicing and then lose to someone who seems suspicious, it can make the experience much less enjoyable.

Despite having great graphics and exciting gameplay, Counter-Strike 2 still has many problems that need to be fixed. If Valve improves the anti-cheat system and matchmaking, the game could become much better. Until then, many players will continue to criticize it and call it one of the most frustrating competitive games available today.

CS2 is just a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circus on wheels. Valve released this piece of ♥♥♥♥, and playing competitive or Premier mode is pure self-torture. Every second match has some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ with a spinbot who doesn't even bother hiding it — he shoots you through the entire map while you're still in spawn. On your team, you either get silent vegetables or screaming ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ who yell into their mics and ♥♥♥♥ all over each other in voice chat instead of playing the game.

And don't get me started on the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ optimization – stutters, crashes, and tickrate that makes your bullets go absolutely nowhere near where you aimed. The only way to play without cheating scum is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Faceit. But that comes with its own pus-filled issues: their janky client, local 'pros' with egos the size of Everest, and you'll still get spam-reported just for landing a headshot through a smoke.

The most ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up part is the player base that crawls into this ♥♥♥♥. On one side — schoolkids with cheats who found some discount software for 3 bucks. They toggle spinbot right in the pistol round, but in voice chat they act all cocky: 'Git gud, old man.' On the other side — dudes in their 30s who should be paying mortgages and taking their kids to kindergarten, but instead they go buy private cheats with their ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ paycheck. Just to boost their Premier rank and feel important somewhere — like measuring ♥♥♥♥♥ with other pathetic old farts. Are you serious, man? You spent your own money on CS hacks instead of actually practicing or just enjoying the game?

And here's a special piece of ♥♥♥♥ — that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Prime upgrade for $15 (or whatever in rubles). I bought it, thinking there'd be clean matches, normal people. But what's the point? ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ NOTHING. The same exact cheaters play on Prime as on free accounts. This garbage gives ZERO advantage — absolutely nothing. You just flushed your money down the toilet. Thanks, Valve.

And on top of this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circus, the skin market is crashing hard. Prices on knives and gloves are tanking because people are fleeing this dumpster fire. Your inventory that you've been building for years is losing value faster than Bitcoin at rock bottom.

But the worst part isn't even the money. I've spent fifteen hundred hours on this game. Fifteen hundred ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hours of my life. And now I clearly realize: that time was completely wasted. No fun, no growth — just constant frustration, cheaters, toxic shouting, and false hopes about the next patch that'll fix everything. It didn't fix a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ thing.

So in the end, we're witnessing a slow but steady decline. From the legendary CS 1.6, where skill and tactics were respected — to this beta version made by some incompetent devs who probably studied at a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ technical school in a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ neighborhood. A place where basic smokes disappear into the floor and the anticheat works on the principle of 'maybe it'll pass.' Valve, go ♥♥♥♥ yourselves with your 'successor.' This isn't CS. This is a piss-stained parody of itself. I regret every single hour and every single dollar. Don't fall for it like I did. This is a dumpster fire. Burn in hell.

Counter-Strike 2 is clearly a successful game and I can see why it has such a dedicated player base. The gunplay is skill-based and rewards precision, but personally I don't enjoy how much the game emphasizes standing still to shoot accurately. I understand it's realistic and adds depth, but it's not my preferred style of shooter.

The game also suffers from issues with cheaters and occasionally annoying teammates, which can make matches frustrating. That said, the competitive gameplay is solid and there's a reason Counter-Strike has remained popular for so long.

Overall, it's a good game, just not one that fits my tastes. 3.5/5.

Counter-Strike is, without a doubt, the greatest game of all time for me. I've been playing Counter-Strike for over 20 years, and no other game has managed to keep me coming back for so long.

Over the years, I've met some amazing people through this game. Some of them became close friends, and a few I even met in real life. Very few games can create connections like that.

The gameplay is still fantastic. The movement feels responsive and satisfying, and the hit registration and feedback are usually instant and accurate. It's easy to get into, but incredibly difficult to master, which is one of the reasons why Counter-Strike remains relevant after all these years.

The maps are excellent, ranging from timeless classics to the occasional new and exciting community-made maps that keep things fresh. The graphics and the Source 2 engine are also a clear improvement and provide a modern look without sacrificing the core Counter-Strike experience.

Would I recommend Counter-Strike 2?

Honestly, I would — if it weren't for several major issues.

The cheating problem remains one of the biggest flaws of the game. Even worse, casual modes such as Deathmatch and Arms Race have become infested with bots to the point where they are practically unplayable at times. The overall selection of casual game modes is also surprisingly limited for a game of this size and age.

What makes this especially frustrating is the impression that Valve is either unwilling or unable to meaningfully address the cheating and bot problems. From a player's perspective, there seem to be very few effective measures being taken, while update after update focuses heavily on new skins and cosmetics.

What started as an incredible local multiplayer experience during my youth now feels like it's slowly crashing into the ground. Meanwhile, players are expected to get excited about the next case, sticker, or weapon finish.

Another thing that has changed for me is the whole case-opening experience. It simply doesn't feel rewarding anymore. What used to be an exciting little bonus has become something I barely care about nowadays. Opening cases no longer gives me the sense of anticipation or satisfaction it once did, making the constant focus on skins and cosmetics feel even more disconnected from the issues that actually affect the gameplay experience.

In the end, Counter-Strike 2 is still an amazing game at its core. The foundation is as strong as ever, and I will continue to play it. However, in its current state, I simply cannot recommend it to new players.

CS2 in 2026 is an absolute joke. It’s infuriating that we’re still dealing with the same fundamental issues that have plagued this game since launch.
  Let’s talk about the elephant in the room: the cheaters. It is frankly pathetic that a multi-billion dollar company like Valve can’t implement an anti-cheat that actually works. If FACEIT can maintain a system that catches 95% of these scumbags, there is zero excuse for VAC to be this useless. Every other lobby in Premier is a coin flip on whether you’re playing a game or watching a spinbotter destroy the lobby. It’s killing the competitive integrity of the game.
  Then there’s the "sub-tick" system. Valve keeps trying to sell this as "better than 128-tick," but it feels like hot garbage. Bullet registration is consistently inconsistent. You’ll have moments where you’re dead on target, spray control is perfect, and nothing hits. The desync is real, the movement feels heavy compared to CS:GO, and the overall "snappiness" that made CS a legend is just gone. 
  Valve is clearly more interested in pushing out skin updates and low-effort patches than actually addressing the core technical debt and the rampant cheating problem. They’ve turned a masterpiece into a chore. If you want to play a competitive FPS, go somewhere else until Valve decides they actually give a damn. Verdict: 2/10. Play it only if you hate yourself.

Title: "What you see is what you get"... if what you want to see is absolute chaos.

Valve promised us the future of tactical shooters, but instead, they deleted CS:GO and handed us a broken tech demo.
​Let’s talk about the "revolutionary" sub-tick system. It’s amazing! I can now peek, run 3 meters behind a solid concrete wall, check my emails, and THEN die to a headshot from someone who didn't even see me on their screen. True magic.
​If you enjoy playing against spin-bots, flying accounts, and wizards who pre-fire you from across the map through three walls, Premier mode is for you. VAC doesn't stand for Valve Anti-Cheat anymore; it stands for Valve Allows Cheaters. The only thing getting banned in this game is my sanity.
​Optimization is a whole different joke. My PC used to run CS:GO at a smooth 300+ FPS. Now, every time a smoke grenade pops, my graphics card sounds like a SpaceX rocket taking off, and my FPS drops to PowerPoint presentation levels. The rubber-banding and random crashes aren't bugs; at this point, they are core gameplay features.
​But hey, at least the weapon finishes look shiny while the game is dying, right?
​Pros:
​Opened my eyes to how peaceful real life is when I uninstall it.
​Cons:
​Everything else.
​Rating: 0/10. Gabe, please sell the IP to a company that actually plays their own game.

★★★★★ Recommended

What light through yonder monitor breaks?
It is the flashbang, and Counter-Strike 2 is the sun.

Good gentles all, lend me thine ears, for I must speak of a marvel wrought not by alchemists nor kings, but by the cunning artificers of Valve. Verily, I did enter this game but seeking brief diversion, and lo, three hundred hours vanished as swiftly as a thief in the night.

Upon these digital fields do warriors gather, armed with rifles, pistols, and the confidence of men who have watched one too many tutorials. Here are forged friendships stronger than steel and rivalries bitter enough to sour the finest wine in Christendom. Never have I witnessed such passion as when five souls unite in pursuit of victory, nor such tragedy as when one fellow insisteth upon buying a Negev every round.

The gunplay? Exquisite. Each shot ringeth with purpose, each duel becometh a test of wit, nerve, and mechanical prowess. To land a perfect headshot is to feel as Caesar crossing the Rubicon; to whiff an entire magazine at point-blank range is to experience Hamlet's despair firsthand.

And what of the smokes? Ah, the smokes! They billow and twist like ghosts upon a moonlit heath, changing the very shape of battle itself. Many a time have I cast one forth with the confidence of a seasoned general, only to discover I had blinded mine own companions and doomed us all. Such is the nature of genius.

The maps stand as great stages upon which this endless drama unfolds. Dust II, ancient and eternal, remaineth the theatre where heroes rise and fools rush through mid with an AWP. Mirage breedeth schemes and stratagems most devious. Inferno turneth every banana into a corridor of fear and flaming death.

Yet no review would be complete without mention of the creatures who inhabit these lands. Some are noble knights who offer wisdom and encouragement. Others possess vocabularies composed entirely of insults and microphone static. Together they form a tapestry most magnificent, a living portrait of humanity in all its glory and madness.

There are moments of triumph so sweet that one feeleth invincible. There are defeats so crushing that one stareth silently at the wall and reconsidereth every life choice that led to queuing for "just one more match." And yet, despite heartbreak, despite despair, despite the teammate who disconnecteth whilst holding the bomb, I always return.

For Counter-Strike 2 is not merely a game. It is a comedy, a tragedy, a history, and at times a psychological experiment. It hath brought me joy, suffering, exhilaration, confusion, and an unhealthy knowledge of smoke lineups.

Would I recommend it?

A thousand times yes.

Though all the world be but a stage, Counter-Strike 2 is the finest one upon which I have ever missed an easy shot.

Hours played: Far too many.
Hours regretted: None.

This game serves as a bleak commentary on human degeneracy. By viewing the community through the lens of the "Dark Triad"—Machiavellianism, narcissism, and psychopathy—we can see these exact malevolent traits reflected in the game's core subcultures: the skins market, toxic player behavior, and the cheating epidemic.

While ordinary players are seduced by the illusion of gambling, the true profiteers of the skin economy are Machiavellian scammers. These actors masterfully manipulate market prices, orchestrate elaborate deceptions, and launder money through digital assets, viewing the community merely as a resource to exploit.

Meanwhile, the immediate toxicity encountered in matchmaking—such as a bottom-fragger lashing out after dropping just two rounds—perfectly reflects the rampant narcissism embedded in the player base. It exposes a fragile ego and an inflated sense of entitlement, where players would rather aggressively tear down their teammates than confront their own inadequacy.

Finally, the surge in blatant cheating—particularly "rage hacking"—is a manifestation of pure psychopathy. These players exhibit a callous disregard for rules, zero empathy for the community, and a malicious desire to ruin the experience for others. By failing to implement a definitive solution, Valve inadvertently provides a playground for this exact brand of antisocial impulsivity.

Counter-Strike 2 is a polished evolution of the classic Counter-Strike formula rather than a complete reinvention. Built on Valve's Source 2 engine, the game features improved graphics, responsive gameplay, updated maps, and dynamic smoke grenades that add new tactical possibilities to every match.

The gunplay remains one of the strongest aspects of the game. Every shot feels impactful, and success depends heavily on skill, positioning, teamwork, and game knowledge. The competitive ranking system keeps matches engaging, while the esports scene continues to attract millions of viewers worldwide.

However, CS2 is not without flaws. Players have reported issues with cheating, matchmaking inconsistencies, and occasional server problems. New players may also find the learning curve steep, as experienced opponents can make the game challenging to get into.

Despite these drawbacks, Counter-Strike 2 remains one of the best tactical shooters available today. It successfully modernizes a legendary franchise while preserving the competitive depth that made Counter-Strike famous. For fans of skill-based multiplayer shooters, CS2 is an easy recommendation.

Counter-Strike 2 is an amazing game to play, especially considering it’s completely free. You can jump in anytime and still get a full competitive experience, which is already a huge plus.

With Prime, the game becomes even more interesting. Over time you can potentially make some profit through weekly drops and cases, and it feels a bit like “cashback” while you play. On top of that, you get to enjoy skins and the whole trading/collecting side of the game, which adds extra motivation to keep playing.

Gameplay itself is very fun and rewarding. You constantly improve your aim, game sense, and overall experience just by playing. It’s one of those games where you actually feel yourself getting better over time.

The only downside is the toxic players — sometimes people can be very loud, aggressive, or spam hate in chat/voice. Luckily, you can just mute or disable chat and focus on the game, which solves most of that issue.

Overall, CS2 is a great competitive shooter: free-to-play, fun, and with Prime it can even feel rewarding financially and progression-wise.

After spending many hours playing Premier in Counter-Strike 2, I can say it's the best competitive mode in the game. The map veto system adds an extra layer of strategy and encourages players to learn more than just a few maps. The CS Rating system is also motivating, giving you a clear sense of progression and something to work toward every match.

Of course, it's not perfect. Matchmaking can sometimes feel unbalanced, with players of different skill levels ending up in the same game. There are also occasional issues with toxic teammates and suspected cheaters. However, when both teams are evenly matched, the matches are intense, rewarding, and incredibly fun.

Overall, Premier offers the most authentic competitive Counter-Strike experience available in CS2. If you enjoy teamwork, strategy, and improving your skills, this mode is definitely worth playing."

I bought Counter-Strike 2 thinking it would be a tactical FPS.
What I got instead was:
a gambling addiction with occasional bomb defusal
5 Russian philosophers arguing over my mother’s profession
one teammate named “toaster oven” who drops 47 kills while eating cereal
and a guy spinning in circles at Mach 5 headshotting everyone through concrete

The matchmaking experience is beautiful. Every game feels like a social experiment funded by the government. One team communicates with military precision while my team has:

a guy with no mic
a guy with too much mic
and someone playing on hotel Wi-Fi from the International Space Station

The economy system teaches valuable life lessons:
“Save this round.”
Nobody saves.
Now we’re all using pistols against fully armed Navy SEALs.

Graphics are incredible though. I can clearly see the exact pixel where my hopes and dreams leave my body after getting one-tapped by a 13-year-old named “tax fraud.”

CS2 is all about figuring out which player is cheating.

Every other match feels like you're starring in a live demo for wallhacks and aimbots. You'll spend more time watching suspicious killcams and checking profiles than actually enjoying the game. The anti-cheat system feels about as effective as putting a "Please Don't Cheat" sign on the front door.

Meanwhile, Valve seems perfectly content collecting money from cases and skins while the player base slowly loses its sanity. The community reports cheaters daily, entire YouTube channels are dedicated to exposing them, and yet the situation somehow keeps getting worse.

Nothing kills my mood faster than knowing that the guy pre-firing every corner with perfect timing might just be running software nobody is stopping. Winning doesn't feel rewarding, and losing is jsut as worse. once you hit gold you get +100 for winning but if you lost taht game -550. You could play 6 games and win all just to lose all the gained points in one game against a cheater.

Unfortunately, all of that gets buried under a mountain of cheaters, questionable matchmaking, and an anti-cheat system that feels permanently stuck in beta or deliberatly turned off to piss us off and take our money.

Counter-Strike 2 had the chance to improve one of the greatest competitive games ever made. Instead, it often feels like Counter-Strike: Cheat Simulator 2.

Would not recommend unless your hobby is wondering whether every good player is legit.

After years of competing at the highest levels of Counter-Strike, one thing has remained constant: no game rewards skill, dedication, and teamwork quite like CS2.

From climbing through the ranks to battling in high-ELO FACEIT and Premier matches, Counter-Strike has always been the ultimate test of mechanical ability, game sense, communication, and mental resilience. Every hour invested is reflected in your performance, and every mistake becomes an opportunity to improve.

What makes CS2 special is that there are no shortcuts. Success is earned through discipline, consistency, and a willingness to constantly evolve. It's a game that has shaped not only my skills as a player but also my understanding of leadership, teamwork, and competition.

Throughout my journey, I've had the privilege of helping newer players develop their game, sharing knowledge, and contributing to a community that continues to push the boundaries of competitive play. Seeing players improve, teams grow stronger, and the scene evolve has been just as rewarding as any individual achievement.

CS2 is more than a shooter. It's one of the few games where mastery is a lifelong pursuit. No matter how much experience you have, there is always another angle to learn, another strategy to perfect, and another challenge waiting around the corner.

Counter-Strike doesn't just create great players.
It creates legends.

Gregory van Der Zalm (Pro CS2 player)

I've put around 65 hours into CS2, and honestly, the game is a frustrating mess.

The core Counter-Strike gameplay is still there. The gunplay feels good, movement is smooth, and when you get a fair match, it's genuinely fun. The problem is that fair matches feel harder and harder to find.

The amount of cheaters I've encountered is ridiculous. I've seen players blatantly cheating, openly admitting they're using hacks, and still playing without any fear of getting banned. Whether it's wallhacks, aim assistance, or other obvious cheats, it completely ruins the competitive experience. Buying Prime doesn't seem to solve the problem either.

On top of that, there are far too many trolls, griefers, and throwers who intentionally ruin matches for everyone else. Nothing kills the enjoyment faster than being stuck in a game with teammates who don't care about winning and are only there to waste everyone's time.

What makes it worse is that Valve's anti-cheat system feels ineffective. It often seems like cheaters can play for long periods without consequences, which makes legitimate players wonder why they should even bother taking matches seriously.

CS2 has the foundation of a great game, but right now it's being dragged down by cheaters, trolls, and poor enforcement. Until Valve properly addresses these issues, it's difficult to recommend.

[h1]COUNTER-STRIKE: GLOBAL OFFENSIVE : THE GAME THAT DEFINED A GENERATION[/h1]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]OVERVIEW:[/h2]

Counter-Strike: Global Offensive is one of those rare games that managed to stay relevant for over a decade without abandoning what made it special in the first place. No abilities, no ultimates, no fancy movement mechanics, just five players, five opponents and a constant battle between aim, teamwork and decision-making.

At its best, CS creates some of the most rewarding moments in multiplayer gaming. At its worst, it reminds you exactly why the mute button exists. Yet somehow, millions of players kept coming back for one more match.

[hr][/hr]

[h3]GAMEPLAY — ★★★★★[/h3]
Simple to learn, endlessly difficult to master. Every bullet matters.

[h3]COMPETITIVE EXPERIENCE — ★★★★★[/h3]
Clutches, comebacks and overtime victories create unforgettable moments.

[h3]MAP DESIGN — ★★★★★[/h3]
Some of the most iconic and replayable maps ever created.

[h3]MULTIPLAYER — ★★★☆☆[/h3]
A mix of incredible teammates and people who should never own a microphone.

[h3]REPLAYABILITY — ★★★★★[/h3]
Thousands of hours later and there's still room for improvement.

[h3]SOUND DESIGN — ★★★★★[/h3]
Every footstep, reload and gunshot carries important information.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]THE BREAKDOWN:[/h2]

[h3]THE GOLD STANDARD OF COMPETITIVE SHOOTERS:[/h3]

CS never relied on flashy mechanics to stay relevant. The game strips shooters down to their purest form: aim, positioning, communication and game sense. Every round feels meaningful because mistakes have consequences. One poorly timed peek or missed shot can completely change the outcome of a match. That simplicity is exactly what made the game timeless.

[h3]THE THRILL OF THE CLUTCH:[/h3]

Few gaming experiences match the pressure of being the last player alive. The bomb is ticking, your teammates are spectating and everyone is watching. Winning a clutch feels incredible because nothing is handed to you. Every decision matters and every successful play feels earned. Some rounds last less than two minutes, yet you remember them for years afterward.

[h3]THE COMMUNITY EXPERIENCE:[/h3]

CS's community is both one of its greatest strengths and biggest weaknesses. Some matches are filled with teammates giving useful callouts, coordinating strategies and encouraging each other after mistakes. Other matches begin with somebody screaming into their microphone before the pistol round even starts. The quality of your experience often depends entirely on the random people you get matched with, but somehow the memorable teammates always keep you coming back.

[h3]MAPS THAT BECAME LEGENDS:[/h3]

Dust II, Mirage, Inferno and Cache became more than battlegrounds. They became part of gaming history. Learning grenade lineups, rotations and common angles slowly transforms confusion into confidence, and the more you learn, the deeper the game becomes. Knowledge is often just as important as mechanical skill, which is why these maps have remained relevant for so many years.

[h3]THE NEVER-ENDING PURSUIT OF IMPROVEMENT:[/h3]

CS constantly gives players something to work on. Better crosshair placement, smarter positioning, cleaner utility usage and faster reactions all contribute to becoming a better player. Even after hundreds of hours, you'll still notice mistakes you made several rounds too late. Improvement becomes addictive because mastery always feels just out of reach.

[h3]SKINS, STATUS AND CULTURE:[/h3]

Skins may be cosmetic, but they became a major part of Counter-Strike culture long ago. From battle-scarred budget loadouts to inventories worth more than entire gaming setups, skins added personality to every match. Whether you cared about them or not, they became impossible to ignore and helped create one of the most recognizable virtual economies in gaming.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]FINAL THOUGHTS:[/h2]

Counter-Strike: Global Offensive earned its place as one of the most influential competitive games ever made. Its mechanics are timeless, its skill ceiling is nearly limitless and its greatest moments remain unmatched. Whether you're winning a clutch, pulling off a perfect execute or getting yelled at by strangers for missing an easy shot, every match creates a story.

[b]The match lasted 40 minutes. The memory lasted years.[/b]

Counter-Strike 2: A Disappointing Update to a Classic

Counter-Strike 2 was one of the most anticipated competitive shooters in recent years, promising improved graphics, better technology, and a fresh experience for fans of the franchise. Unfortunately, the game often feels more like an unfinished replacement for Counter-Strike: Global Offensive than a true step forward.

One of the biggest criticisms I have of Counter-Strike 2 is that it removed features and content that players had enjoyed for years in CS:GO. At launch, several maps and game modes were missing, making the game feel less complete than its predecessor. Many players expected a significant upgrade, but instead found themselves with fewer options.

The game's technical performance has also been a source of frustration. While the new graphics engine can make maps look more detailed, some players experienced lower frame rates and performance issues compared to CS:GO. In a competitive shooter where every millisecond matters, these problems can negatively affect gameplay.

Another major complaint is the matchmaking and anti-cheat system. Cheating has remained a concern within the Counter-Strike community, and many players feel that the game's anti-cheat measures have not improved enough. Facing suspected cheaters can make competitive matches feel unfair and discourage long-term play.

Counter-Strike 2's new "sub-tick" system was advertised as a revolutionary improvement for responsiveness, but many players reported inconsistent experiences. Instead of feeling like a clear advancement, the system became a topic of debate, with some arguing that gameplay felt less predictable than expected.

The game can also be criticized for relying heavily on the formula established by previous Counter-Strike titles. While maintaining a classic style is not inherently bad, Counter-Strike 2 introduces relatively few groundbreaking innovations. For some players, it feels more like a graphics update than a true sequel.

Conclusion

Counter-Strike 2 succeeds in modernizing the visual presentation of the series, but it falls short of the high expectations surrounding its release. Missing content, performance concerns, ongoing cheating complaints, and a lack of major innovation make the game feel underwhelming. While dedicated fans may still enjoy its competitive gameplay, critics could argue that Counter-Strike 2 is a frustrating example of a sequel that does not do enough to justify replacing the game that came before it.

I really wanted to enjoy this game, but after spending a lot of time with it, I can honestly say it has been one of the most frustrating gaming experiences I have had. The game has potential, and that is probably the worst part, because you can clearly see that it could be something much better. Instead, it feels abandoned, unfinished, poorly managed, and focused more on taking money from players than actually improving the experience.

One of the biggest problems is the FPS and performance bugs. The game runs terribly far too often, even on systems that should be able to handle it. Random frame drops, stuttering, freezing, lag spikes, and unstable performance make the game feel broken. It is extremely annoying to lose fights or fail objectives not because of skill, but because the game suddenly decides to drop frames or freeze at the worst possible moment. Performance problems have existed for a long time, and yet they still feel like they have not been properly fixed.

Another major issue is that the developers do not seem to care enough about the actual problems players are reporting. Updates either take too long, ignore the biggest issues, or focus on things that do not really improve the core gameplay. Players have been asking for basic fixes for years, but many of the same problems are still there. It gives the impression that feedback is being ignored unless it is related to something that can make the game more money.

The lack of a proper anti-cheat system is also unacceptable. Cheaters ruin matches, destroy the competitive experience, and make honest players feel like they are wasting their time. A game like this needs strong anti-cheat protection, but instead, it feels like cheating is not taken seriously enough. When players constantly run into suspicious behavior and nothing meaningful happens, it makes the whole game feel unfair and pointless.

Player support is another huge disappointment. When something goes wrong, it feels like there is nobody there to actually help. Reports, complaints, and support requests often feel ignored or handled with generic responses. Good player support is important, especially in a game with so many bugs and issues, but this game makes players feel like they are on their own. If the developers want people to keep playing, they need to treat the community with more respect.

The maps are also a serious problem. Some maps still feel unfinished even after many years. There are areas that look incomplete, poorly designed, unbalanced, or simply neglected. After this much time, there is no excuse for maps to still feel like early-access content. Players should not have to wait year after year for basic improvements that should have been completed a long time ago.

Worst of all, the game gives the impression that it only cares about money. Instead of fixing FPS bugs, adding proper anti-cheat, improving support, finishing maps, and listening to players, the focus often seems to be on selling more paid content, cosmetics, passes, or other monetized features. It feels like the game is treated more like a cash machine than a project that the developers truly care about improving.

Overall, this game is disappointing because it could have been great, but it is held back by poor performance, lack of anti-cheat, weak player support, unfinished maps, and developers who seem more interested in money than fixing the actual game. I cannot recommend it in its current state. Until the serious problems are fixed and the developers start listening to the community, this game deserves criticism.

I have been playing Counter‑Strike since the days of CS:Source, back when the community was fun, the toxicity was at least creative, and you didn't need a 500€ knife to feel like you belonged. I moved through CS:GO (non‑prime, the wild west of cheaters and chaos) and I actually enjoyed it because at least the matchmaking had a sense of humor. Then CS2 arrived – shiny new engine, subticks, better graphics, and for a moment I thought maybe, just maybe, Valve had finally fixed the game. Spoiler: they did not. The matchmaking is a joke that keeps repeating itself – me, with my 5K elo, getting teammates who are either AFK, arguing about pistol round kills, or playing with their feet, while the enemy team looks like they just qualified for a major. I lose multiple games and in the fourth match, after two AFKs and two children screaming at each other, I finally snap – I kill the AFKs with a molotov and get a 7 day ban. Not them, of course. Me. Because apparently griefing is punished, but wasting other people's time is completely fine. The anti‑cheat is a ghost, ranking up takes a geological era, and every game makes me more angry than the last. So I'm done. I'm selling my inventory, uninstalling the game, and putting my energy into something that doesn't make me want to throw my monitor out the window – like playing other games or creating them. At least that project doesn't crash, flame me, or put five-year-olds on my team. CS2 is not recommended for anyone who values their sanity, their time, or their ability to feel joy.

It’s alright. Sometimes it really feels like you’re playing against cheaters, but with CSRep you can check whether there’s a higher chance the players are legit or cheating.

CS2 right now isn’t a game, it’s a psychological experiment Valve is running to find out how long the human nervous system can last before voluntarily switching to farming in Minecraft. It’s like a competitive FPS and an anxiety attack simulator had a child. It’s full of cheaters, so sometimes the real question isn’t whether you’re playing well, but whether the enemy can see through walls or is just “having a good day.” Half the matches already feel suspicious enough that instead of a killcam, you could film a documentary called “How NOT to cheat in an obvious way.”

Matchmaking: Matchmaking is its own universe. With 5k ELO, you can easily get thrown in with 9–10k players. It’s like riding a bicycle against Formula 1 drivers, while the system pats you on the back saying, “this will be good practice for you.” The ELO system also has a special reward: if you leave a match because it’s literally unplayable, you get a nice big penalty, as if you were the problem — not the fact that you’re currently in a troll festival or cheater simulator.

Death behind the wall DLC :
New CS2 feature: Are you behind cover? You die.

The hit registration and subtick system sometimes seem to have a life of their own. You see the headshot, you feel it, you’re already about to celebrate, and then the game says no. The enemy turns around and takes you out with a single shot.

Teammates: Team spirit sometimes is like a fragile IKEA piece of furniture: in theory it holds together, in practice it falls apart after the first round.

The best combination: someone who teamkills + trolls + flames all at once. That’s a full event. Teamkilling is one of the most honest moments in the game. There are times when you get “accidentally” headshotted in spawn, and your teammate just writes in chat: “mb”...

Voice chat sometimes feels like a mix between a family argument, a rap battle, and a panic attack: “REPORT HIM” “STFU YOU BOT” “I AM CARRYING” while someone is breathing into the mic like Darth Vader.

The best part is when someone tries to find out who’s to blame after every round. If you win: they carried. If you lose: everyone else is a “bot.” If it’s a draw: somehow you’re still the problem.

And of course there is always that one guy who is AFK, but occasionally comes back just to ask “eco?” before disappearing again, as if he just checked in to see if you’re still suffering.

The most brutal part is that every match has one common element: five strangers, all completely convinced that it’s the other four who are losing the game.

200+ hours in and I can finally understand why Counter-Strike has remained popular for so many years.

CS2 is simple on the surface—plant the bomb, defuse the bomb—but the skill ceiling is incredibly high. Every match feels different, and there is always something to improve, whether it's aim, positioning, utility usage, or teamwork.

The graphics and lighting look great in Source 2, and getting that clean headshot never gets old. Premier can be frustrating at times, but winning a close match with friends makes it all worth it.

Still learning, still improving, and still queueing for "one more game."

200+ hours played and I'm nowhere near mastering it. Recommended.

Lag.  Terrible rubber banding even though I'm hardwired, 24 ping, and never experience this kind of connectivity issue in other games except ones that are well known to have networking issues.  The number of times I see someone shoot and they completely miss the other player but still hit is unbelievable.  I played 10's of thousands of hours of 1.6 and never had such bad issues even on 56k.  Where you shot was where the bullet went and it was incredibly rare to see object placement discrepancies due to ping - and when you did see them, it was far less egregious.

The crosshair bounces all over the place making it highly difficult to place follow up shots quickly or manage the recoil pattern as the screen shakes preventing reliable prediction.  Follow recoil, don't follow recoil, they're both pretty terrible to play with.  Especially against people who have played with it for thousands of hours and are used to it.  Some people would call it a skill gap - I would call it making the game harder to play than necessary.

Weapon balancing is awful.  The m4a1 has a 20 round clip?  I thought the trade off on silence was the dink with armor vs. the ak 1 shot.  The negev is way too accurate over range.  The auto shotgun has the same problem.  I've seen way too many across a bombsite headshot kills in 1-2 shots with it.  It's like there is no real spread and you're shooting slugs...but your not.  Tech9 is way op too...i've seen 1 shot headshots up close with it, the fire rate is way to high and the recoil far too little.  It's way better than pretty much any other pistol because of how easy it is to use.  Only the deagle (maybe r8 but why is there an r8?) beats it over range...and the deagle (along with plenty of other guns) have a strange bullet spread over distance that make them unwieldly.   Though this might be due to the lag issue mentioned above.

Quite frankly, I don't know why they added so many more weapons and changed the standing weapon characteristics when the balancing was pretty much perfect in 1.6.  I also don't know how the internet has gotten better but this game has gotten worse.

On top of these core issues that are impossible to fix at this point (I would guess since they haven't been in the case of lag - and it's not like they can change weapons since it would mess with the skin market) there are things they can fix but don't.

1 - Match results screen is not skippable.  This means most times you don't get to vote on the next map.  God forbid you level up prime and then you never see the vote screen.  To think I paid extra money for this feature is ridiculous.

2 - There are a large number of maps missing from the original game, leaving the map pool quite small.  Estate, prodigy, assault, aztec, dust, cbble are all missing.  I'm also missing at least 2 that I can't remember off the top of my head what they're names were.

3 - There is a serious lack of game modes.  Free for all deathmatch is the only one?  Why not team?  Why is gungame only on community maps?  Why are all the servers in the browser locked or fake w/ 250 players?  I'd be willing to bet custom servers don't grant you xp either.  IMO match making killed what helped make CS great to begin with.  

4 - Plan to spend at least 1000 dollars if you want to pull your own knife.  I've spent about 250 and only ever gotten 1 pink - wait, is black lotus pink?  Forget about red and golds I guess huh?  I've watched peanut pull probably over 1k of cases and he has similar luck - though he just got a knife for the first time not in trade up.  And I haven't watched him play that much CS either.  You're just better off buying a skin if you want something which is what I ended up doing for a fraction of the cost.  IDK how there are so many kids playing this game since gambling is such a core part of it's ecosystem.  It's such a terrible experience too, but then again I don't like to gamble.

5 - Speaking of kids, the community (due to match making and not server based anymore) is toxic.  The number of people abusing others, calling them slurs, targeting women and just being bad people in general is crazy.

CS2 is truly a masterpiece. Nothing builds competitive spirit quite like loading into a match and watching a guy named ‘totally_legit_player123’ prefire your entire team through three walls before warmup even ends. Every game feels like a thrilling boss fight against someone whose gaming chair clearly costs $9.99/month with auto-renewal.

The anti-cheat system is also incredibly immersive because it lets you experience what it’s like to be hunted by the FBI while armed with only a Glock and optimism. It really keeps you sharp. Why practice recoil control when your opponent can calculate your exact location from another dimension?

Overall, CS2 is less of a tactical shooter and more of a social experiment testing how long humans can endure psychological warfare before uninstalling. 10/10 — best game if you’ve ever wanted to see whether raw skill can overcome a guy spinning at Mach 3 with wallhacks and aimlock.

The New Gold Standard for Competitive Tactical Shooters!
With over 100 hours clocked in Counter-Strike 2, this game successfully carries the CS:GO legacy into the next generation with highly noticeable improvements.

Revolutionary Smoke Mechanics: The new volumetric, responsive smoke in Source 2 is a complete game-changer. The way smoke interacts with bullets and HE grenades makes team strategies way more dynamic.

Crisp Visuals and Audio: The engine upgrade makes classic maps look incredibly clean, vibrant, and well-lit. The updated audio cues for footsteps and gunfire are highly precise, which is crucial for those intense clutch situations.

Rock-Solid Gunplay: The iconic recoil handling of weapons like the AK-47 and M4 remains familiar, yet everything feels snappier and incredibly satisfying when you land that perfect headshot.

If you are looking for a tactical shooter that purely tests your raw skill, aim, and teamwork, CS2 remains the undisputed king of the genre. Highly recommended! 9/10.

I downloaded CS2 expecting a game i will have fun in, just like in CSGO. What I got was couples therapy for five people who just met and already hate each other.


What VALVE promised:

"Sub-tick precision"
"Enhanced graphics"
"Improved smoke physics"


The reality:

Getting headshot by someone who pre-aimed your ancestors' ancestors.
A teammate who got no armor, no util and a Negev because "trust me bro".
Smokes so immersive that you, your enemies, and any sense of hope are just not there.

The economy system deserves its own paragraph. You can spend 23 rounds playing textbook CS: utility, trades, map control, the whole thing, then watch one guy forcebuy a P90 and somehow win a 1v3 while bunny-hopping. The game then rewards him and punishes you. Valve has PhDs working on this.
Ranking works like so: top frag, clutch the round, hit every shot, remember your teammates' birthdays, donate to charity, achieve inner peace and still drop 400 ELO because someone disconnected after dying to his own molotov in round two.


The five stages of a match:

1.Hope
2.Suspicion
3.Open accusations
4.Four people blaming the fifth guy (who has not said a single word throughout the whole match)
5."One more"

Every. Single. Time.
10/10. I am not okay. See you in ten minutes.

[u][b]Pros:[/b][/u]

[b] No Kernel Anti-Cheat [/b] - My biggest praise is that Counter-Strike does NOT use a Kernel Anti-Cheat, Valve is one of the only companies making the choice to respect player's ownership over their systems. 

[b] Less Moderated [/b] - Instead of nitpicking and trying to hyper-moderate everyone's speech in game, Valve gives players tools and agency to decide for themselves, from what I understand there is still some automated systems but they appear to be very lenient. This is a much better approach than other competitive games out there, that have taken overly aggressive measures to control speech based on subjective moral nonsense.

[b] Nice Graphics [/b] - The first thing I noticed when I started playing CS2 was how improved the graphics are from previous iterations. CS in general was always a game that felt graphically primitive compared to other FPS games, which was never a bad thing necessarily, but I was quite impressed how well they've been able to retain the feeling of the core game while lifting it up visually as well.

[u][b]Cons:[/b][/u]

[b] Lack of Incentive & Reward [/b] - CS2 does NOT reward or incentivize players to win, and it doesn't give players desirable or meaningful rewards to represent achievements, exceptional skill, or dedication.

[b] Skins Feel Worthless & Undesirable [/b] - I've always seen a lot of chatter and bravado about skins in Counter-Strike, and while I'm not against the idea of having a monetary-based model around aesthetics, I've come to the conclusion that they have no real value in relation to the game because they're exclusively monetary in nature. The way to solve this would be to have exclusive in-game skins that only represent achievement, exceptional skill, or dedication. Currently, I have no desire to own or display a skin because they represent nothing of value to me.

[b] Creative Expression has been Restricted & Commodified [/b] - Sprays, Animated Profile Pictures, and Nicknames should be open to free player customization in official servers, while this is partly a criticism of Steam, it effects CS2. Instead Sprays and Profile Pictures have been commodified, and nicknames reflect on your entire Steam account. Counter-Strike Nicknames should exist on their own isolated layer. I understand that abuse of this expression is inevitable, but that can be solved by giving players options to disable them, or only to show officially approved sprays for example.

[b] No Solo-only Queue [/b] - Premades present a variety of problems in all modes, a solo-only option should be provided for players who have no interest in playing around premades.

[b] Sensory Problems & Accessibility [/b] - This is relatively minor, but I personally suffer from a hearing impairment problem, and would very much appreciate to see some visual aids added to the game, if possible.

Counter Strike is a beautiful and groundbreaking game created by the legendary Saint Alexander, whose vision and talent changed gaming forever. Through his dedication and creativity, he crafted an intense tactical shooter that combines teamwork, strategy, and skill in a way that keeps players engaged for countless hours. The game quickly became a global phenomenon, attracting players of all ages who were drawn to its exciting gameplay and competitive spirit.

A major contributor to the game's success was Tom Pearl, the brilliant digital designer who helped bring Saint Alexander's vision to life. Through his exceptional attention to detail and artistic talent, Tom Pearl created the visual identity that made Counter Strike stand out from other games. His work helped shape the game's environments, interface, and overall presentation, giving players an immersive experience that felt both realistic and exciting.

One of the greatest achievements of Saint Alexander was his ability to create a game that is easy to understand but incredibly difficult to master. Every match requires players to communicate effectively, work together as a team, and make important decisions in a matter of seconds. This balance between accessibility and depth has allowed Counter Strike to remain popular for many years and has helped build one of the strongest gaming communities in the world.

The maps designed under Saint Alexander's leadership and refined through Tom Pearl's digital design expertise are considered masterpieces by many fans. Every location feels carefully crafted, offering unique strategic opportunities while maintaining a strong visual identity. The combination of thoughtful gameplay design and outstanding artistic direction helped create memorable battlefields that players continue to enjoy.

Saint Alexander and Tom Pearl formed a remarkable creative partnership. While Saint Alexander focused on developing the core gameplay and competitive experience, Tom Pearl ensured that every visual element matched the high standards of the project. Their combined talents resulted in a game that feels polished, engaging, and timeless.

Counter Strike also became a cornerstone of competitive gaming. Professional tournaments attracted talented players from around the world, while spectators were captivated by the intense action and strategic depth. The foundation established by Saint Alexander and enhanced by Tom Pearl's design work helped transform the game into a worldwide phenomenon that continues to inspire players and developers alike.

Today, Counter Strike stands as a testament to the extraordinary work of Saint Alexander and the outstanding digital design of Tom Pearl. Together they created a gaming masterpiece that has influenced generations of players and left a lasting mark on the history of interactive entertainment. Their dedication, creativity, and passion ensured that Counter Strike would be remembered as one of the greatest games ever made.

Counter-Strike 2 is a landmark upgrade to one of the greatest FPS franchises ever made. The move to Source 2 brings stunning visual clarity, responsive hit registration, and sub-tick networking that makes every shot feel genuinely earned. The smoke grenade overhaul alone is a game-changer — dynamic, reactive smokes that interact with bullets and grenades add a whole new layer of tactical depth that veteran players will love.
The Premier mode with its MMR-based matchmaking finally gives competitive play a meaningful ranking ladder, and the map pool stays tight and iconic.
On the Ban System:
Valve's VAC Live system is a step up — bans happen faster than the old wave-ban model, and the new VAC bans are permanent and non-negotiable, which is the right call.
The honest caveat though —
VAC's cheat detection is still inconsistent. Blatant spinbotters sometimes survive multiple reports for weeks, and subtle wall/aim assist cheaters can fly under the radar entirely since VAC relies heavily on behavioral pattern detection rather than real-time monitoring. The Overwatch system being gone doesn't help either.

This game is a far cry from what CS:GO was in the past, but CS:GO wasn't perfect either. It had its own problems, like cheaters and bad voice chat moderation, but it also had a better engine (Source 1), faster movement, and better shot registration. If Valve focused on gameplay before skins and cosmetics, it is entirely possible for CS2 to be amazing with just a few adjustments.
It's frustrating to spray down a player, run behind a wall, and still die because of the 64-tick (subtick) system—it's bull**** and not fun. Visually, the game looks amazing, and I'm running 200+ FPS even with OBS and Firefox open, but they can still do so much better. In CS:GO, movement was indeed way faster, more skillful, and fun to use; here, it feels "noobified," slow, and built for newer players. Of course, new players are what keep a game alive, but this approach backstabs the older fanbase that has been here since 1.6.

The only thing I feel they did somewhat right was the empty magazine reload update, since it forces you to remember not to "Call of Duty reload." Other than that, Valve focuses too much on skins, and it feels like everything else is being thrown away or discarded. To be fair, I only started playing a year and a half before writing this review, so I only played a little bit of CS:GO. Still, GO felt much better—though not by a massive milestone like some people claim.
If they fix the anti-cheat, hit registration, and movement, I think more people will call this their "forever game." I hope Valve fixes these issues and rethinks the future of Counter-Strike altogether. We are not just numbers for your monthly income; we are people, and we should be heard and listened to more often. Without that connection, this game will become just like Overwatch 2: a game past its prime and heading downhill.

Only game that can make you feel like a tactical genius and a broke idiot at the same time. One minute you’re clutching a 1v5 with a perfectly timed AWP shot that would make your ancestors proud, the next you’re staring at your Steam inventory wondering why you just spent $400 on a skin for a gun you only use when you’re drunk. This game isn’t just a shooter — it’s a high-stakes casino where the house always wins and the prize is slightly better looking pixels.
The gameplay is still pure crack. The gunplay feels crisp, the movement is buttery, and there’s nothing quite like hearing “Bomb has been planted” while your heart tries to escape your ribcage. I love the satisfaction of out-aiming some Russian kid who’s been playing since he was in diapers, or perfectly executing a smoke lineup that makes your teammates call you a god. CS2 turned the classic formula into something modern and beautiful… until you remember the real meta isn’t aim — it’s not opening cases.
This game is an absolute money pit. Valve looked at my bank account and said “challenge accepted.” I’ve dropped more money on cases, stickers, and operation passes than I care to admit. Every time I tell myself “this is the last case,” I end up spending another $80 chasing that dream knife that I’ll probably never pull. My inventory is worth more than my car, and the only thing it does is sit there looking pretty while I still get destroyed by default skins. The skin economy is so predatory it makes loot boxes look like charity.
And don’t get me started on the community. You’ve got the toxic gods screaming in voice chat, the guys who play 40 hours a week and still call everyone “bot,” and the occasional god-tier player who makes you question your entire existence. But somehow it’s all worth it when you top-frag and your friends spam “NICE” in chat like you just won the lottery.
CS2 has me in a chokehold. It’s the only game where I can lose $200 in virtual skins and still say “worth it” after one good match. The addiction is real, the dopamine hits are nuclear, and my financial advisor is crying in the corner.

⭐ 0/10 — The Cheaters Have Better Matchmaking Than Players

Counter-Strike 2 feels less like a competitive shooter and more like a social experiment designed to test how much suffering a player can endure before uninstalling.

The anti-cheat situation is so bad that every match feels like a coin flip between playing Counter-Strike and starring in a live demonstration of "How Walls Work." Trust Factor? Nobody knows what it does anymore. Good players get punished, questionable players get rewarded, and everyone gets thrown into the same digital dumpster fire.

Then there are the servers. Nothing says "premier competitive experience" quite like dying three business days after reaching cover. Every gunfight feels like you're arguing with the server about what actually happened.

And let's talk about communication from Valve. The community reports problems for months, and the response often feels like a mysterious silence broken only by another case release. Bugs survive longer than most esports careers.

As for Gabe Newell, at this point I picture him as a giant greedy fox sitting on a mountain of case keys, occasionally peeking down at the player base and saying, "Have you considered opening another crate instead of fixing the game?"

CS2 somehow took one of the greatest competitive games ever made and turned it into a trust-factor lottery running on servers powered by wishful thinking.

Recommended? Only if your hobbies include packet loss, existential despair, and trying to determine whether your opponent has incredible game sense or just a second monitor showing the entire map.

Counter-Strike 2 is truly a masterpiece of modern gaming. There’s honestly nothing more satisfying than loading into Premier, winning a few rounds, and then getting instantly headshotted through three walls by a level 2 account named “xXPutinLover69Xx.” The adrenaline rush of losing 400 ELO to someone spinning at Mach 5 while VAC quietly observes in spiritual silence is an experience no other game can replicate.

Speaking of VAC — wow. What an incredible anti-cheat system. I especially appreciate how it gives cheaters enough time to fully explore their creativity before considering any action. Why ban them immediately when they can ruin hundreds of matches first? Truly player-first design philosophy.

One of my favorite aspects of CS2 is the cultural enrichment. Before playing, my Russian vocabulary was limited to “vodka.” Now, thanks to my incredibly friendly teammates, I can confidently recognize phrases like “Cyka Blyat,” “idi nahui,” and several other beautiful expressions screamed directly into my ears at 140 decibels after losing pistol round. It’s basically free language lessons.

The matchmaking experience is also second to none. Every game feels like a social experiment. Will your teammates communicate? Will they buy rifles? Will they understand what a bombsite is? Nobody knows. Sometimes I get paired with tactical masterminds who rush mid with a Nova every single round while blasting music through their microphone. The unpredictability keeps the game fresh.

And let’s not forget the community. Nothing builds character faster than being told to uninstall by someone who finished the game 4-19 while permanently flashbanging the team. The emotional growth I’ve experienced from these interactions has been invaluable.

Performance-wise, CS2 runs amazingly too. I especially enjoy how every update introduces exciting new FPS drops and mysterious stutters. It’s like opening a loot box, except the reward is input lag.

Overall, Counter-Strike 2 is more than just a game. It’s a test of patience, sanity, and emotional stability. Whether you’re losing ELO to blatant cheaters, learning Russian profanity from your teammates, or questioning your life choices after queueing “just one more game” at 2AM, CS2 always delivers a memorable experience.

Great memes.
Questionable humanity.

Even in deathmatch, there are tons of case-farming bot accounts that kick real players out instantly. There are also lots of people using cheats and multiple accounts, who kick players out the instant they join. Then when you get kicked out and try to join another server, you can't play at all because Valve always tries to put you in the server you just got kicked from, and then after wasting your time waiting for it to load, you get an error message saying you can't join the server. Bots, cheaters, and anyone who wants to, can literally prevent you from playing the game at all, until the server they kick you from closes. Another big issue is that there are so many toxic players in this game who will hurl slurs at you just for being inexperienced, or for no reason at all. I had to turn off the chat because of this. Reporting does nothing. Valve, as a multi-billion dollar company, you can do better. You should be investing in some decent, real human moderation instead of donating 20 million dollars to AI slop. You should be banning bots, cheaters, and those who harass and bully others. But you won't because you only care about money. The option to vote players out should be removed, especially in deathmatch. It is way too prone to abuse and prevents new players like me from getting better at the game. Some people would respond to that with "What about cheaters?". The answer is that this game wouldn't have so many cheaters if Valve actually had decent moderation. Innocent players should not be punished just because Valve has terrible moderation. At this point, I'm only playing this game to get some Steam credit from item drops, as I don't have much money, and I want to save up for some games to take my mind off of things and hopefully play with some friends someday. This game could be fun with some friends, but as an inexperienced player trying to play by myself, it's really not enjoyable.

If you're looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if you're looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.

Ingredients:

1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter
2. 1/2 cup white sugar
3. 1/2 cup brown sugar
4. 1/4 cup water
5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon
6. 1 pinch salt
7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced
8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie

Directions:

1. Preheat oven to 425 degrees F (220 degrees C).
2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside.
3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices.
4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal.
5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust.
6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C), and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.

Counter-Strike 2 is still one of the most fun and competitive FPS games ever made. The gunplay is great, the maps are iconic, and there really isn't another game that scratches the same itch. When you get a good match, it's easy to see why people have been playing Counter-Strike for over 20 years.

The biggest problem is the cheating situation. VAC feels like it only works when someone is blatantly rage hacking and making it obvious. Meanwhile, there are plenty of players, including Prime accounts, who try to hide their cheats and make them look legitimate. Anyone who has spent enough time in Premier or Competitive knows exactly what I'm talking about.

What's frustrating is that Valve is a billion-dollar company with more than enough resources to improve anti-cheat, yet it often feels like the issue isn't a priority. Legitimate players are left questioning whether someone is just having a good game or quietly using assistance that never gets detected.

CS2 is still a great game at its core, but it's sad to see it in its current state. The potential is there, the gameplay is there, but the cheating problem continues to hold it back.

In my opinion, Valve has lost interest in CS2.

While Deadlock receives major gameplay updates, reworks, new mechanics, and constant developer attention, CS2 feels abandoned.

The cheating problem continues to frustrate players, trust in VAC is at an all-time low, and many long-time fans feel ignored.

What makes it even worse is the growing feeling that Valve cares more about monetization than about the actual game. Some players believe that Valve would prefer more skin trading to happen directly through the Steam Market, where they collect fees, rather than through third-party marketplaces. Whether that's true or not, the lack of communication and focus on core issues has created a lot of distrust within the community.

Counter-Strike used to be Valve's flagship competitive title. Today it feels like the game exists to generate revenue while anti-cheat, content updates, and player concerns take a back seat.

Meanwhile, Deadlock gets the attention, innovation, and development effort that CS2 players have been asking for.

[h1]CS2 WILL WASTE YOUR TIME[/h1]

[h2]THE LEGACY[/h2]
Counter Strike... The name itself evokes a particular feeling depending on who you are. If you've never touched the game and want to begin, the name won't end up living up to the legacy you've heard about.

1.6, source, csgo... These games are what made Counter Strike the sport it is today.

[b]Cs2 on the other hand is a state of a game![/b]

[h2]The Cheater Problem[/h2]
You WILL absolutely find a cheater almost every match. You have a greater chance they are on the enemy team as you occupy one of the 5 players on your side. So yes, the game will actually be rigged against you. Plenty of players cheat subtly, and a decent portion blatantly cheat without shame shooting you through smokes, aim botting, and wall hacking. These cheaters have been at it for years and no sign of any bans. Every so often you might hear of a purge, VAC bans hitting thousands of accounts at once, but don't be fooled. They will be back immediately.

It get's even worse! [b]Cheats are made to make people rich![/b]

The deathmatch servers are ridden with automated bot accounts playing with cheats to farm skins. The economy of the CS market has attracted leeches. If you engage with any trading system online a scammer will try to get in touch with you at least once a day. The greed that encompasses this game actually gives me a sickening feeling in my gut. It's disgusting.

[h2]Why Is The Game Still Popular?[/h2]
[i]Cs2 is hated by the majority but still popular[/i]

Despite the cheating problem many people still play the game... Why?

Either they're addicted to the skins, the gambling, or elo grinding. 

Or they simply fell in love with what counter strike is, but I assure you even they aren't happy with Cs2. It's sounds are off, the movement is sluggish and floaty in comparison to the beloved CSGO (giving the offensive a gigantic advantage), grenades throw late and far too slow, alias binds and optimisation has been removed from the game, the list goes on. No one likes the state of Cs2, not even the pros.

[h2]There Is No Placement For New Players[/h2]
The skill ceiling is so gigantically high and the matchmaking ranks are terribly designed that you'll have a terrible experience working for an accurate rank. With 1.2k hours in this game I am still considered the lowest rank, and I have spent at least a third of this time grinding out training and watching demos back. 

I have learned crosshair placement, counter strafing, angles, map timing, utility line ups, spray control patterns, spray transfers, tap shooting, burst shooting, crab walking, and plenty of strategies. Despite all I have learned I often bottom frag, losing duels to worse players who can run and shoot with a rifle worrying little about their spray and accuracy. 

I can't explain it but it actually feels like RNG about 50% of the time. If you have a bad game, it's not likely going to get better, you'll feel as if some players have a luck on their side whilst you have none.

[h2]Is My Complaints Valid?[/h2]
Maybe all of this sounds like a mental issue? Yes and no. You need to fight confidently for a good chance at winning, a bad mindset can literally lose you the entire game and downgrade the performance of your team mates. The reason why I partially say no is because I have walked into this game confident, with a healthy mindset and relentlessly kept it despite having such a rough time. But to no avail I can lose so hard even with confidence in my abilities, and consistently lose game after game.

You could sink thousands of hours into this game and get utterly nowhere, remain in the worse rank, have terrible stats and nothing to show for your work because this game favours spray and praying, and it also has a terrible ranking system meaning that decent players with over 3 thousand hours could be in your low rank. In fact it's common that even in the low elo ranks you'll die the millisecond your tiny head appears on the enemy screen, it doesn't matter how fast you're moving or if you learned to stop pressing "w" as you strafe.

Anyone who knows this game reading my review might see this is "skill issue" and they'd be right. I'm terrible at this game, bottom of the barrel. And it's not uncommon for people like me with 1k hours to be this bad, I'd say the majority of players with this amount of time are this bad. The game is so unbelievably hard. That would be fine if there was something worthwhile for the time and effort. But there isn't.

If you're on a losing streak you will absolutely feel like breaking something. The game recognises your losing streak and decides to take away 500 elo per loss and give back 100 per win due to this. It's designed to throw you so far back so quickly and destroy everything you've worked so hard for. It could have taken 500 hours to learn and improve to properly rank up, and in hours lose it all.

I've had weeks where every game all I got was a 4 v 5 team due to a leaver, a blatant enemy cheater, or a team mate who has never even touched a shooter before. And it's not uncommon to experience this.

[h2]In Summary[/h2]
I love counter strike, but I hate Cs2. I even hate Faceit as this seems to be only slightly better but far more toxic and mentally draining. I could sink in more hours to get better but my time and effort is better put elsewhere. At minimum you want about 2k hours before you could even learn the "basics" of this game and become a reliable player with consistent performance.

If you want to give this game a go, by all means go for it. But you will have many REAL LIFE days ruined literally due to a video game. If you're naturally gifted mechanically at shooters on the mouse and keyboard, maybe you'll have a better experience, I have friends that have had a much better and smoother experience due to their gifts, and have swiftly put in 8k hours (I am not saying they haven't had to struggle to get where they have got to, but their gifts have undoubtedly persuaded them to continue playing after 1k hours). I on the other hand personally can't bare another 1 thousand hours to just be called "average", or worse yet still be considered "bad" by the majority of players.

If you like competitive games but can't handle the mental strain of terrible ranking systems, unfortunately I think you're out of luck, no game seems to have it. CS2 being one of the worst I've seen.

If you came to read negative reviews because you're mad. Hi, turn from competitive games, it only hurts your pride, seek Jesus instead, He will give more than this game will ever have to offer. Love to all. If you do decide to play this game, good luck and I truly hope you have a better experience than me.
